meta.focus: Full na-adjective conjugation paradigm: before nouns (～な), adverb form (～に), te-form connector (～で), and polite conjugations. Easily confused na-adjectives (きれい, 有名, 嫌い). Plus こと as a nominalizer: turning verb phrases into nouns, Xのことが好き (holistic liking), and ～たことがある (experiential 'have done').

sections.title: na-Adjectives & こと — Two Patterns in One Lesson
sections.summary: This lesson covers two key grammar areas: the full na-adjective conjugation paradigm, and こと — a small but powerful word that turns verbs into nouns. Together they expand the range of sentences you can build dramatically.
sections.whyItMatters: な-adjectives are extremely common in everyday Japanese. Words like きれい (beautiful), 有名 (famous), and 嫌い (disliked) are na-adjectives — master the pattern once and every na-adjective follows it. こと then lets you nominalise any verb phrase ('eating', 'going to Japan', 'having seen something'), unlocking natural expressions like 'I like eating' and 'Have you ever been to Japan?' These two tools together take your Japanese from simple to expressive.
youWillLearn: Before nouns: きれいな 花 (beautiful flower) — add な before the noun
Polite present: きれいです (is beautiful)
Polite past: きれいでした (was beautiful)
Polite negative: きれいじゃないです / きれいではありません (is not beautiful)
Polite past negative: きれいじゃなかったです (was not beautiful)
て-form connector: きれいで (beautiful and…) — use で to chain adjectives
Adverb form: きれいに (beautifully / in a clean way) — modifies verbs
⚠ Watch out: きれい, 有名, 嫌い LOOK like i-adjectives but are NOT. Never say きれいい or きれいくない!
こと: plain form verb + こと turns the verb into a noun phrase (食べること = 'eating')
Xのことが好き: liking X as a whole — deeper and more encompassing than plain が好き
～たことがある: express past experience — 'I have (ever) done ~'

sections.title: na-Adjective Conjugation Chart
sections.description: な-adjectives conjugate by adding endings after the base form (the word itself, without な). The key connector is で — used for the past tense of the copula and the て-form connector.

rows.label: Before noun
cells: きらいな たべもの

rows.label: Polite negative
cells: ゆうめいじゃないです

rows.label: Polite past negative
cells: きれいじゃなかったです
ゆうめいじゃなかったです
きらいじゃなかったです

notes: The base form of a na-adjective is the word itself (きれい, 有名, 嫌い). Add endings after the base.
Before nouns: always add な. きれい + な + 花 = きれいな 花. Forgetting な is one of the most common errors.
The te-form is で, not くて. This is the key difference from i-adjectives: 高い → 高くて (i-adj), きれい → きれいで (na-adj).
The adverb form is に, not く. This is also different from i-adjectives: 大きく 書く (write largely), きれいに 書く (write neatly).
Polite negative: きれいじゃないです and きれいではありません are both correct. じゃない is the spoken/casual form; ではない is more formal/written. Both are standard.

pattern.label: LOOKS LIKE い-adj
pattern.text: きれい / ゆうめい / きらい

sections.meaning: Easily confused na-adjectives — they look like i-adjectives but are not
sections.explanation:
Some of the most common Japanese adjectives look like い-adjectives because their base form ends in い — but they are actually な-adjectives. This means they conjugate completely differently.

**The three most common traps:**

| Word | Meaning | Type | Correct past | ✗ Wrong past |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| きれい | beautiful / clean | **na-adj** | きれいでした | ~~きれいかったです~~ |
| ゆうめい | famous (有名) | **na-adj** | ゆうめいでした | ~~ゆうめいかったです~~ |
| きらい | disliked (嫌い) | **na-adj** | きらいでした | ~~きらいかったです~~ |

**How to spot a na-adjective:**
- Before a noun, it uses な: きれいな 花 (beautiful flower), 有名な 人 (famous person)
- An i-adjective uses nothing before a noun: 大きい 花 (big flower), 新しい 人 (new person)
- If you need to add な before a noun, it's a na-adjective!

**The かわいい trap:** かわいい (cute) ends in い but IS a genuine い-adjective. So: かわいかったです (was cute) is correct. Do not confuse it with きれい.

**The ～そうです trap:** きれい + そうです = きれいそうです (looks beautiful) ✓. But かわいい + そうです = かわいそうです — and this is a completely different word meaning 'pitiable / poor thing'! So avoid 「かわいそうです」 to mean 'looks cute'.
notes: Memory trick: if the adjective can take な before a noun (きれいな, 有名な, 嫌いな), it's a na-adjective and uses で/でした/じゃない for conjugation.
きれい is unusual because it is a na-adjective even though it ends in い. It was originally a noun-like word (清い = clean/pure), not a true い-adjective.
有名 (ゆうめい) is a compound of 有 (exist) + 名 (name), meaning literally 'having a name' — names make you famous. This compound structure explains why it behaves like a noun/na-adj rather than an i-adj.
The かわいそう trap is important for natural Japanese. If you want to say 'that puppy looks cute', say 「あの 子犬は かわいいですね」or avoid the そうです form entirely with かわいい.

parts.gloss: was beautiful (na-adj past: きれい + でした)

examples.breakdown: きれい (na-adj) + でした = きれいでした. NOT きれいかったです — that would be an i-adjective form and is wrong.

parts.text: ゆうめいじゃないです。
parts.gloss: is not famous (polite negative: ゆうめい + じゃないです)

examples.breakdown: ゆうめい (na-adj, written ゆうめい until kanji are taught) negative: ゆうめい + じゃないです = ゆうめいじゃないです. Compare with i-adj: 大きい → 大きくない (not big). Na-adj uses じゃない, i-adj uses くない.

sections.meaning: Chaining adjectives — くて (i-adj) and で (na-adj)
sections.explanation:
When you want to describe something with two or more adjectives ('the flowers are beautiful and cute'), you connect them using the て-form. The te-form depends on the adjective type:

| Adjective type | て-form | Example |
|---|---|---|
| い-adjective | stem + くて | 大きい → 大きくて (big and…) |
| な-adjective | base + で | きれい → きれいで (beautiful and…) |
| い-adjective irregular | よく + て | いい → よくて (good and…) |

**Pattern for chaining:**
[First adj — te-form] + [Second adj — final form]

```
花は きれいで、かわいいです。
(The flowers are beautiful and cute.)

魚は 大きくて、おいしそうです。
(The fish is big and looks delicious.)

空気が よくて、気分が いいです。
(The air is nice and I feel good.)
```

Only the **last** adjective carries the full ending (です or ない). All adjectives before it use their て-form.
notes: You can chain more than two adjectives: きれいで、かわいくて、小さい 花 (beautiful, cute, and small flower). The last adjective takes the final form; all others use て-form.
If you mix i-adj and na-adj in a chain, remember: i-adj uses くて, na-adj uses で. Example: 大きくて、きれいな 川 (a big and beautiful river).
The て-form connector means 'and' in a neutral, non-contrasting sense. For contrast ('but'), use が after the full form: きれいですが、小さいです (it's beautiful, but small).
When the te-form connector appears before a noun phrase (not a copula), the last modifier needs な: きれいで、かわいい 花 (works) vs きれいで、かわいい (predicate, also works).

parts.gloss: beautiful and… (na-adj te-form: きれい + で)

examples.breakdown: きれい (na-adj) → きれいで (te-form) + かわいい (i-adj) → かわいいです (polite final form). The first adjective takes the te-form, the last takes the full ending.

parts.gloss: big and… (i-adj te-form: 大き + くて)

examples.en: The fireworks are big and beautiful.
examples.breakdown: 大きい (i-adj) → 大きくて (te-form) + きれい (na-adj) → きれいです (polite). Mixed i-adj + na-adj chain: first uses くて, second uses full form.

parts.gloss: nice and… (いい irregular te-form: よ + くて)

examples.en: The air is nice and my mood is good.
examples.breakdown: いい (irregular i-adj) → よくて (て-form, よ- stem). This links two independent clauses through the te-form connector. The pattern 「AがBくて/でCがDです」 is extremely natural in Japanese.

sections.meaning: Plain form + こと — turns a verb phrase into a noun ('the act of ~')
sections.explanation:
こと is a special noun meaning 'thing / matter / fact' (always written in hiragana, never as 事 in modern usage). When placed after a **plain form verb**, こと converts the entire verb phrase into a noun that can then fill any noun role in a sentence — subject, object, or topic.

**The pattern:**
```
[plain form verb phrase] + こと
食べること  (eating / the act of eating)
日本に行くこと  (going to Japan)
花火を見ること  (watching fireworks)
```

**In a full sentence:**
- 食べることが好きです (I like eating)
- 日本に行くことが夢です (Going to Japan is my dream)
- 花火を見ることが楽しいです (Watching fireworks is fun)

**Key point:** The verb before こと is always in its **plain form** (dictionary form for present; more on past below). Even in a polite sentence, the verb inside こと stays plain — politeness comes from the ending (です, ます) after こと.

More advanced こと patterns (ことができる for ability, ことにする for decisions) are taught in N4 grammar lessons.
notes: こと is always written in hiragana — 事 (the kanji) exists but is not used in grammar contexts.
The verb immediately before こと must be in the plain/dictionary form: 食べること ✓, 食べますこと ✗.
こと is different from もの (physical object) and の (softer nominalizer used in spoken language). For grammar patterns, こと is the standard choice.
Reminder: こと is available from N5.9 as an early-use word — always write it in hiragana.

parts.gloss: going to the mountains (nominalised by こと)

examples.en: I like going to the mountains.
examples.breakdown: 行く (dictionary form) + こと = 行くこと (the act of going). が marks it as the subject/object of 好きです.

parts.text: 花火を 見ることが

examples.breakdown: 見る (dictionary form) + こと = 見ること. The whole phrase 花火を見ること acts as the subject of 楽しいです.
食べる (dictionary form) + こと = 食べること (eating as a concept). Simple and extremely common — you will use this pattern all the time.

sections.meaning: Xのことが好き — liking X as a whole, holistically
sections.explanation:
You already know Xが好き (like X). Adding の + こと changes the nuance from 'like the specific thing X' to 'like everything about X' — a deeper, more encompassing fondness.

| Pattern | Nuance | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 日本が好きです | I like Japan (the country, as a direct object) | Simple preference |
| 日本のことが好きです | I love Japan / everything about Japan | Broader, deeper feeling |

The pattern Xのことが好き is especially natural with **people**: it implies you like someone as a whole person, not just a surface quality.

**More examples:**
- この川のことがすきです → I love this river (holistically)
- りきぞさんのことが好きです → I like Rikizo (as a person)
- 音楽のことが好きです → I love music (everything about it)

Note: すき uses hiragana because the kanji 好 is not taught until N4.
notes: The の in Xのこと is the possessive/connector particle (taught in G2). こと here means 'the matter/existence of X' — so Xのこと = 'the matter of X / everything about X'.
In everyday speech, Xのことが好き vs Xが好き sounds very similar, but native speakers feel the difference. Xのことが好き tends to sound more heartfelt.
You can also use Xのことが好きじゃない (I don't like X) or Xのことが嫌い (I dislike X) following the same pattern.

parts.gloss: like / fond of (な-adj: すき + です)

examples.en: I love Japan / I love everything about Japan.
examples.breakdown: 日本 + の + こと = 'the matter/essence of Japan'. が marks it as what's liked. すきです = polite 'like'.
この川 + の + こと = 'everything about this river'. A warmer, more encompassing statement than この川がすきです.

sections.meaning: ～たことがある — 'have (ever) done ~'; expressing past experience
sections.explanation:
This is one of the most useful patterns in Japanese. ～たことがある lets you say whether or not you have ever had a particular experience.

**The pattern — always plain past (た-form) before こと:**
```
[verb plain past] + こと + が + ある/あります
花火を見たことがあります  (I have seen fireworks [before])
日本に行ったことがあります  (I have been to Japan)
```

**Negative — replace ある with ない:**
```
[verb plain past] + こと + が + ない/ありません
食べたことがありません  (I have never eaten [it])
```

**Question — add か at the end:**
```
[verb plain past] + こと + が + ありますか
日本に行ったことがありますか  (Have you ever been to Japan?)
```

**Critical rule:** The verb before こと is ALWAYS the **plain past** (た-form), even in polite sentences. The politeness comes from ます/ません at the very end. Never say ~~行きますことがあります~~ — that is wrong.
notes: Think of ～たことがある as 'I have a record/experience of having done ~'. こと here = 'the experience of'.
The past tense of this pattern (I had done it before a certain point) uses ことがあった: 子どものとき、山に登ったことがありました (When I was a child, I had climbed a mountain).
Don't confuse with ～ことがある (dictionary form + ことがある), which means 'there are times when ~'. That is a separate, more advanced pattern.
This pattern pairs naturally with: 一度 (once), 何度も (many times), 一度も～ない (not even once).

parts.gloss: the experience of having seen (plain past: 見た + こと + が)

examples.breakdown: 見た (plain past of 見る) + こと + が + あります. The plain past た is key — this is a fixed construction.

parts.gloss: the experience of having gone (行った + こと + が)

examples.en: Have you ever been to Japan?
examples.breakdown: 行った (plain past of 行く) + こと + が + ありますか = 'Do you have the experience of having gone to Japan?' This is the standard 'Have you ever ~?' pattern in Japanese.

parts.gloss: the experience of having eaten (食べた + こと + が)
does not exist (polite negative: ありません)

examples.breakdown: 食べた (plain past of 食べる) + こと + が + ありません. Swap あります → ありません for negative. This is 'I do not have the experience of having eaten it.'

sections.title: na-Adjectives in Context — Reading a Sentence
sections.sentence: 花は きれいで、かわいくて、空気も きれいに なりました。
sections.translation: The flowers are beautiful and cute, and the air also became clean.

parts.gloss: beautiful and… (na-adj て-form: きれい + で)
cute and… (i-adj て-form: かわい + くて)
cleanly (na-adj adverb: きれい + に)

sections.breakdown: This sentence uses three different adjective forms: (1) きれいで = na-adj て-form, linking to the next; (2) かわいくて = i-adj て-form, continuing the chain; (3) きれいに = na-adj adverb form modifying なりました. Notice how な-adj uses で for the te-form and に for the adverb — while い-adj uses くて and く.

sections.title: かいわ: きれいな 空と 花 (The Beautiful Sky and Flowers)
sections.context: Miki and Ren discuss the flowers and goldfish they see near the river after the rain.

lines.jp: 雨の 後、空が きれいに なりましたね。
lines.en: After the rain, the sky became beautiful, didn't it.

lines.jp: ええ。空気も きれいで、気分が いいですね。
lines.en: Yes. The air is also clean and I feel good, don't I.

lines.jp: 川に きれいな 花が あります。
lines.en: There are beautiful flowers by the river.

lines.jp: 金魚も います。きれいで、かわいいですね。
lines.en: There are also goldfish. They're beautiful and cute, aren't they.

lines.jp: あの 花火は きれいそうですね。大きくて、きれいです。
lines.en: Those fireworks look beautiful. They're big and beautiful.

lines.jp: ええ！今日は きれいな 日ですね。また 来たいです。
lines.en: Yes! Today is a beautiful day, isn't it. I want to come again.

lines.jp: ここで 花火を 見たことが ありますか。
lines.en: Have you ever seen fireworks here?

lines.jp: ええ、あります。この 川の ことが すきです。
lines.en: Yes, I have. I love everything about this river.

sections.title: na-Adjective Conjugation Practice
sections.instructions: Convert each adjective to the target form. Watch out for な-adjective vs い-adjective differences.

items.hint: na-adj polite: base + です = きれいです
na-adj て-form: base + で = きれいで. NOT くて — that's for い-adj!
na-adj plain negative: base + じゃない = きれいじゃない. NOT きれいくない!
na-adj adverb: base + に = きれいに. NOT きれいく — that's for い-adj!
かわいい is a GENUINE い-adj! Remove い → かわい(stem) + くて = かわいくて
かわいい is an い-adj: remove い → かわい(stem) + くない = かわいくない

sections.title: G11 Practice — na-Adjective Forms
sections.instructions: Choose the correct answer for each question.

items.q: Which of the following is a な-adjective (not an い-adjective)?
items.explanation: きれい is a な-adjective. Despite ending in い, it uses な before nouns (きれいな 花) and で for the て-form (きれいで). かわいい, 大きい, and 長い are genuine い-adjectives that conjugate with -く endings.

items.q: How do you say 'beautiful flowers' in Japanese (adjective before noun)?
items.explanation: な-adjectives add な before a noun: きれい + な + 花 = きれいな 花. Forgetting な is the most common error. い-adjectives don't need anything: 大きい 花 (big flower), no の or な needed.

items.q: How do you connect two adjectives: 'beautiful and cute' — きれい and かわいい?
choices: きれいで、かわいいです。
きれいくて、かわいいです。
きれいにて、かわいいです。
きれいて、かわいいです。
items.answer: きれいで、かわいいです。
items.explanation: な-adjective て-form is で: きれいで. い-adjective て-form is くて: かわいくて. Since きれい is a な-adj, the connector is きれいで. Then かわいいです as the final form (い-adj).

items.q: How do you say 'the flowers became beautiful'? （花が きれい___ なりました）
items.explanation: な-adjective adverb form: base + に. きれい + に + なりました = 'became beautiful'. Compare i-adj: 大きく なりました = 'became big' (く). The pattern Xになる means 'to become X'.

items.q: What is the polite past tense of きれい (was beautiful)?
choices: きれいくなかったです
items.explanation: な-adjective polite past: base + でした = きれいでした. NEVER きれいかったです — that would be an い-adjective past and is wrong. The copula でした is used for both nouns and な-adjectives in the past tense.

choices: きれいじゃなかったです
きれいに なりました
items.explanation: きれいかったです is WRONG — きれい is a な-adjective, not an い-adjective. The correct past tense is きれいでした. The other three forms are all correct: でした (past), じゃなかったです (past negative), に なりました (became, adverb + naru).

items.q: Which sentence correctly uses こと as a nominalizer?
choices: 山に 行くことが 好きです。
山に 行きことが 好きです。
山こと 行くが 好きです。
山が こと 行き 好きです。
items.answer: 山に 行くことが 好きです。
items.explanation: The pattern is: [plain form verb phrase] + こと. 行く (dictionary form) + こと turns 'go to the mountains' into a noun phrase. が marks it as the subject/object of 好きです. The verb must be in plain dictionary form before こと — 行きこと (using the masu-stem) is wrong.

items.q: What does「花火を 見たことが あります」mean?
items.explanation: ～たことがある expresses past experience: 'have (ever) done ~'. 見た (plain past of 見る) + こと + が + あります = 'I have the experience of having seen fireworks'. The plain past た-form before こと is the key — it is always the past form here, even in a polite sentence.

items.q: How is「日本のことが好きです」different from「日本が好きです」?
choices: のことが好き expresses a broader, more encompassing fondness for Japan as a whole.
のことが好き is past tense; が好き is present tense.
のことが好き is more formal; が好き is casual.
They mean exactly the same thing with no difference.
items.answer: のことが好き expresses a broader, more encompassing fondness for Japan as a whole.
items.explanation: Xが好き means 'I like X' — direct preference for the specific thing. Xのことが好き means 'I love everything about X' — a holistic fondness covering all aspects. のこと adds the nuance of 'the whole matter/essence of X'. In practice, Xのことが好き sounds warmer and more heartfelt.
